a:r.a:t: This flow shows what happens when a user asks a broad, corpus-scale question like "Which systems have supply chain vulnerabilities across all programs?" The router detects broad research intent -- keywords like "across all," "which systems," and the sheer query length push the complexity score above 3, which triggers deep RAG mode. The system in deep_rag.py computes a complexity score from multiple factors: word count over 24 adds points, "across" and "entire" add a broad-research bonus of 2, and citation-related keywords add another point. When the score crosses the threshold, the policy engine sets search_mode to "deep" and enables up to 4 reflection rounds with 3 to 5 parallel retrieval lanes. The research_coordinator takes over because it has rag_researcher in its worker pool -- something the regular coordinator lacks. The rag_researcher agent is a ReAct-mode specialist with 17 tools including the RAG Workbench trio: search_corpus_chunks for direct vector and keyword retrieval at the chunk level, grep_corpus_chunks for regex pattern matching across raw chunk text, and fetch_chunk_window for pulling surrounding context around a specific chunk ID. Meanwhile, graph_manager queries the Neo4j-backed knowledge graph using one of three GraphRAG methods -- local, global, or drift search -- to find entity relationships like "System A depends on System B which has vulnerability CVE-2024-1234." The graph evidence and RAG evidence are assembled together through handoff artifacts. The verifier then checks whether every claimed vulnerability has a source citation and whether all systems from the graph were covered. If coverage gaps exist, a revision round sends the planner back with specific instructions like "System C appeared in the entity graph but was not analyzed." This can loop up to 4 times. The result is a comprehensive vulnerability map that combines document evidence with structural knowledge graph relationships -- something neither RAG nor GraphRAG could produce alone.
